prompt: Construct a MedOps Integrated Facility Platform supporting offline inpatient operations, clinical documentation, and on-site dining programs for a mid-sized US hospital. The Dioxus web UI runs on the hospital intranet and guides three primary roles: Administrators configure systems and security, Employees (nurses, unit clerks, dietitians, cafeteria managers) execute daily workflows, and Members (patients or approved staff) place meal orders. Employees can search and maintain patient profiles with demographics, allergies/contraindications, medical history, and visit notes, with a clear timeline view that highlights what changed since the last revision and requires a reason-for-change for clinical edits. Attachments such as imaging exports, prescriptions, and consent forms are uploaded through a drag-and-drop panel with instant file-type validation (PDF/JPG/PNG only) and a 25 MB per-file limit. For inpatient logistics, staff manage building–unit–room–bed assignments in a visual bed board where each bed follows a state machine (Available, Reserved, Occupied, Cleaning, Out of Service) and every check-in, check-out, transfer, and bed swap captures who performed it and when. Cafeteria managers use an admin console to create dishes with categories, options (sizes, flavors, add-ons), photos, publish/unpublish controls, sold-out toggles, time-slot selling (for example Breakfast 6:00–10:00 AM, Lunch 11:00 AM–2:00 PM), and ranking rules that influence on-screen recommendations. Members can start or join department group-buy meal campaigns (for example, “Cardiology Lunch Run”) with a configurable success threshold (such as 10 orders by 10:30 AM); campaigns auto-close after 30 minutes of inactivity, and orders show a clear status path including create, billed, canceled, and credited, with split tickets by pickup point or kitchen station and staff-entered order notes that are visible in an operations history panel. The backend is implemented in Rust using Rocket to expose REST-style intranet APIs consumed by the Dioxus frontend, with MySQL as the single local system of record for all clinical, operational, and ordering data. Authentication is strictly offline using username and password only; passwords must be at least 12 characters with complexity rules, accounts lock for 15 minutes after 5 failed attempts, sessions expire after 8 hours of inactivity, and Administrators can disable accounts immediately. RBAC is enforced at both menu and API levels so that, for example, cafeteria staff cannot access medical records, and clinical users cannot edit pricing or inventory. Data governance is built in via tiered storage tables for raw ingested data, cleaned/validated data, and analytics outputs, with metadata and lineage recorded for each transformation and a retention policy of 7 years for clinical records unless superseded by local policy; deletion requests follow a compliant “tombstone plus audit” approach to preserve required audit trails. Sensitive fields (such as SSNs when stored, allergy lists, and clinical notes) are encrypted at rest using application-level encryption keys stored locally, and UI displays mask highly sensitive identifiers by default. Operational audit logs are append-only and cover record access, edits, exports, and order actions. For internal data ingestion, a configurable collection task manager supports seed URLs on the intranet, extraction rules (CSS/XPath/JSONPath/regex), pagination and depth strategies, incremental fields, scheduled runs (for example daily at 2:00 AM), and task versioning with one-click rollback. Finally, an offline experimentation layer standardizes event instrumentation across UI and API calls, computes funnels and retention for dining and workflow adoption, tracks recommendation KPIs like CTR and conversion, and allows controlled A/B tests or multi-armed bandits with feature backtracking and version tracking, all computed locally without any third-party services.
